codeReview.summary: Feature was described as validation-only but uncovered and fixed a real bug: QEMU enumerates virtio-mmio devices in reverse command-line order, so with both virtio-keyboard-device and virtio-tablet-device present, the keyboard ends up on the second input channel (handle 4) while the tablet occupies the first (handle 1). The Ctrl+Tab context-switching logic previously only ran on the first channel, causing it to silently fail. The fix correctly duplicates the Ctrl+Tab detection, modifier tracking, IMAGE_MODE toggle, scroll offset save/restore, content surface re-render, and title bar re-render onto the second input channel's MSG_KEY_EVENT handler. The worker also correctly gates non-modifier key forwarding to editor-only in text mode. Full demo flow was verified via 4 QEMU screenshots (boot → type → Ctrl+Tab to image → Ctrl+Tab back with text preserved). All 921 tests pass. The fix is correct and complete for its scope.

issues.description: ~60 lines of Ctrl+Tab context-switch logic are copy-pasted between the first input channel handler (line 1617) and the second input channel handler (line 1697). The worker acknowledged this in discoveredIssues and suggested extracting a process_key_event() helper. This duplication creates maintenance risk — if context-switch behavior changes, both copies must be updated in lockstep.

Only KEY_LEFTCTRL (keycode 29) is tracked as a modifier on both channels. KEY_RIGHTCTRL (keycode 97) is not handled. This is a pre-existing limitation consistent across both channels, not introduced by this change, but worth noting since users with different keyboard layouts may expect Right Ctrl to work.

sharedStateObservations.observation: QEMU enumerates virtio-mmio devices in REVERSE command-line order. This means the last -device flag on the command line gets the highest MMIO address, which the DTB enumerates first. When virtio-tablet-device is added after virtio-keyboard-device, the tablet becomes input_devices[0] (handle 1) and the keyboard becomes input_devices[1] (handle 4). This caused Ctrl+Tab to fail silently because the compositor only checked the first channel. The architecture.md mentions the tablet is a 'second virtio-input device' but doesn't document the reverse enumeration behavior that determines which device ends up on which channel.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Worker spent ~15 tool calls and tried multiple debugging approaches (HMP sendkey, QMP input-send-event, VNC backend, debug prints) before identifying the root cause via serial log analysis of 'device 0: id=18' / 'device 1: id=18' and init's handle assignment logic. This knowledge would have saved significant debugging time if documented.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The duplicated input channel handling (~60 lines of identical Ctrl+Tab logic) suggests a convention gap: when handling events from multiple input channels, shared event-processing logic should be extracted into a helper function rather than copy-pasted between channel handlers.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: system/services/compositor/main.rs lines 1605-1674 (first channel) vs lines 1689-1744 (second channel) — nearly identical code blocks for Ctrl tracking, Ctrl+Tab detection, IMAGE_MODE toggle, surface re-render, and title bar update.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The os-worker skill's Ctrl+Tab testing guidance says 'Send sendkey ctrl-tab via QEMU monitor' but this was insufficient — the worker had to spend extensive time debugging why sendkey wasn't working before discovering it was a device ordering issue, not a QEMU routing issue. The skill could note that when virtio-tablet-device is present, device enumeration order changes and both input channels must handle keyboard shortcuts.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript shows ~20+ tool calls debugging the Ctrl+Tab failure before root cause identification. skillFeedback.suggestedChanges mentions this: 'AGENTS.md note about pointer assertions being verified via code-review only should also mention that keyboard modifier keys (Ctrl+Tab) may have QEMU routing issues in headless mode that are only discoverable through actual QEMU testing.'

summary: PASS. The worker discovered and correctly fixed a real bug where QEMU's reverse virtio-mmio device enumeration placed the keyboard on the second input channel when virtio-tablet-device was present, causing Ctrl+Tab context switching to silently fail. The fix duplicates the Ctrl+Tab handling onto both input channels. The code is correct, all 921 tests pass, and the full demo flow was visually verified via 4 QEMU screenshots. Two non-blocking issues noted: the duplicated logic should be refactored into a shared function (worker acknowledged this), and only Left Ctrl is tracked (pre-existing). Key knowledge gap: QEMU's reverse device enumeration order should be documented in architecture.md to prevent similar debugging sessions.
